content:
5. Building the World Beneath the Story (The Iceberg Method: Character Work, Theme, and Setting)
6. Scene Work: The Pulse of a Story (Positive and Negative Shifts, Emotional Waves)
7. Momentum and Refinement (Writing, Rewriting, and Structuring for a Graphic Novel)
8. Your Creative Roadmap (How to Move from Exploration to Execution)

SECTION 1: Understanding the Foundations of a Great Story

Before we analyze specific works or begin crafting ideas, let’s define the core principles that shape a strong narrative.

1.1 A Story Is About Change Through Conflict

At its heart, every story is about a protagonist undergoing transformation due to an antagonistic force rising in their life.
• A character must be making choices. Every scene should involve decisions and consequences.
• Conflict isn’t just about fights—it’s resistance. This could be an internal struggle, an external rival, or a system that opposes the protagonist.
• Antagonism isn’t just the villain. It’s any force that disrupts the protagonist’s normal world and forces them to adapt.
